title: Dog Chemical Burn in Eye: Emergency Care & First Aid Guide
meta_desc: A chemical burn in your dog's eye is a medical emergency. Learn immediate first aid steps and why you must contact a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: A chemical burn in a dog's eye is a critical medical emergency that requires immediate flushing with water and urgent veterinary care to prevent permanent blindness.

What exactly is a chemical burn in a dog's eye?

A chemical burn occurs when an irritating or corrosive substance—such as household cleaners, fertilizers, or automotive fluids—comes into contact with the sensitive tissues of the eye. These substances can cause rapid damage to the cornea and surrounding tissues.

Is a chemical eye burn in a dog considered a veterinary emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-level medical emergency as chemical exposure can cause permanent blindness or the loss of the eye within minutes or hours.
  • You must flush the eye immediately with sterile saline or plain water.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian right away.

How can taking a photo help the veterinarian triage my dog?

  • While your priority is flushing the eye, sending a photo to your veterinarian or a triage service can help professionals assess the severity of the irritation and the specific area affected.
  • A clear image allows the vet to prepare the necessary treatments before you even arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of chemical burns to a dog's eye, immediate emergency care involves ocular irrigation with physiologic saline for 10-20 minutes, followed by fluorescein staining to detect corneal injury. Ocular exposures may result in epiphora, blepharospasm, eyelid edema, and/or corneal ulceration, requiring thorough flushing of the affected area with tepid water or physiologic saline, followed by monitoring for the development of ocular irritation or ulceration. Systemic signs should be treated symptomatically.

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology, Dermatology, Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3001)

faqs.question: What should I use to flush my dog's eye?
faqs.answer: Use sterile saline solution if available; otherwise, lukewarm tap water is acceptable in an emergency to dilute the chemical.

faqs.question: How long should I flush the eye?
faqs.answer: You should aim to flush the affected eye continuously for at least 15 to 20 minutes before heading to the veterinary clinic.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops for the burn?
faqs.answer: No, do not use any medicated human eye drops or ointments unless specifically directed by a veterinarian, as some ingredients can worsen chemical damage.
